title: BiKA: Kolmogorov-Arnold-Network-inspired Ultra Lightweight Neural Network Hardware Accelerator
summary: Lightweight neural network accelerators are essential for edge devices with limited resources and power constraints. While quantization and binarization can efficiently reduce hardware cost, they still rely on the conventional Artificial Neural Network (ANN) computation pattern. The recently proposed Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) presents a novel network paradigm built on learnable nonlinear functions. However, it is computationally expensive for hardware deployment. Inspired by KAN, we propose BiKA, a multiply-free architecture that replaces nonlinear functions with binary, learnable thresholds, introducing an extremely lightweight computational pattern that requires only comparators and accumulators. Our FPGA prototype on Ultra96-V2 shows that BiKA reduces hardware resource usage by 27.73% and 51.54% compared with binarized and quantized neural network systolic array accelerators, while maintaining competitive accuracy. BiKA provides a promising direction for hardware-friendly neural network design on edge devices.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces BiKA, a lightweight neural network design for edge devices that significantly reduces hardware resource usage while maintaining competitive accuracy.
AI.motivation: The need for efficient and lightweight neural network accelerators for resource-constrained and power-limited edge devices.
AI.method: BiKA replaces nonlinear functions of Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks with binary thresholds, enabling a computation pattern that uses only comparators and accumulators.
AI.result: BiKA achieves a 27.73% to 51.54% reduction in hardware resource usage compared to binarized and quantized neural network accelerators, with competitive performance, as tested on an FPGA prototype.
AI.conclusion: BiKA offers an extremely efficient architecture for neural networks, suitable for hardware-friendly deployment on edge devices.

title: FPPS: An FPGA-Based Point Cloud Processing System
summary: Point cloud processing is a computational bottleneck in autonomous driving systems, especially for real-time applications, while energy efficiency remains a critical system constraint. This work presents FPPS, an FPGA-accelerated point cloud processing system designed to optimize the iterative closest point (ICP) algorithm, a classic cornerstone of 3D localization and perception pipelines. Evaluated on the widely used KITTI benchmark dataset, the proposed system achieves up to 35$\times$ (and a runtime-weighted average of 15.95x) speedup over a state-of-the-art CPU baseline while maintaining equivalent registration accuracy. Notably, the design improves average power efficiency by 8.58x, offering a compelling balance between performance and energy consumption. These results position FPPS as a viable solution for resource-constrained embedded autonomous platforms where both latency and power are key design priorities.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FPPS, an FPGA-accelerated system that speeds up point cloud processing for autonomous driving by up to 35x while improving power efficiency.
AI.motivation: Autonomous driving systems face computational challenges and energy constraints in processing point clouds for real-time applications. The paper aims to address these through performance optimization and energy efficiency.
AI.method: The authors developed FPPS, an FPGA-accelerated system, to optimize the iterative closest point (ICP) algorithm, ensuring efficient 3D localization and perception.
AI.result: On the KITTI benchmark dataset, FPPS delivers up to 35x speedup (15.95x average) over CPU baselines and enhances power efficiency by 8.58x while maintaining registration accuracy.
AI.conclusion: FPPS effectively combines high computation speed and energy efficiency, making it suitable for embedded autonomous systems constrained by latency and power demands.

title: GenDRAM:Hardware-Software Co-Design of General Platform in DRAM
summary: Dynamic programming (DP) algorithms, such as All-Pairs Shortest Path (APSP) and genomic sequence alignment, are fundamental to many scientific domains but are severely bottlenecked by data movement on conventional architectures. While Processing-in-Memory (PIM) offers a promising solution, existing accelerators often address only a fraction of the work-flow, creating new system-level bottlenecks in host-accelerator communication and off-chip data streaming. In this work, we propose GenDRAM, a massively parallel PIM accelerator that overcomes these limitations. GenDRAM leverages the immense capacity and internal bandwidth of monolithic 3D DRAM(M3D DRAM) to integrate entire data-intensive pipelines, such as the full genomics workflow from seeding to alignment, onto a single heterogeneous chip. At its core is a novel architecture featuring specialized Search PUs for memory-intensive tasks and universal, multiplier-less Compute PUs for diverse DP calculations. This is enabled by a 3D-aware data mapping strategy that exploits the tiered latency of M3D DRAM for performance optimization. Through comprehensive simulation, we demonstrate that GenDRAM achieves a transformative performance leap, outperforming state-of-the-art GPU systems by over 68x on APSP and over 22x on the end-to-end genomics pipeline.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces GenDRAM, a PIM accelerator for dynamic programming tasks, which achieves significant performance improvements by addressing traditional bottlenecks and leveraging monolithic 3D DRAM.
AI.motivation: Dynamic programming tasks like APSP and genomic sequence alignment suffer from data movement bottlenecks in conventional architectures. Existing PIM systems address these partially, causing system-level inefficiencies. Therefore, a full-stack, integrated approach is necessary.
AI.method: The authors propose GenDRAM, a PIM accelerator that integrates the entire data pipeline for tasks like genomics onto a single M3D DRAM chip. It uses specialized Search Processing Units and universal Compute Processing Units, along with a 3D-aware data-mapping strategy.
AI.result: GenDRAM significantly outperforms current GPU systems, achieving over 68x speedup on APSP tasks and 22x on end-to-end genomics workflows, based on simulations.
AI.conclusion: GenDRAM demonstrates the transformative potential of PIM accelerators in handling data-intensive dynamic programming workloads, resolving key performance and system bottlenecks.

title: LeGend: A Data-Driven Framework for Lemma Generation in Hardware Model Checking
summary: Property checking of RTL designs is a central task in formal verification. Among available engines, IC3/PDR is a widely used backbone whose performance critically depends on inductive generalization, the step that generalizes a concrete counterexample-to-induction (CTI) cube into a lemma. Prior work has explored machine learning to guide this step and achieved encouraging results, yet most methods adopt a per-clause graph analysis paradigm: for each clause they repeatedly build and analyze graphs, incurring heavy overhead and creating a scalability bottleneck. We introduce LeGend, which replaces this paradigm with one-time global representation learning. LeGend pre-trains a domain-adapted self-supervised model to produce latch embeddings that capture global circuit properties. These precomputed embeddings allow a lightweight model to predict high-quality lemmas with negligible overhead, effectively decoupling expensive learning from fast inference. Experiments show LeGend accelerates two state-of-the-art IC3/PDR engines across a diverse set of benchmarks, presenting a promising path to scale up formal verification.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces LeGend, a machine learning-based method to improve inductive generalization for formal verification in IC3/PDR engines by using one-time global representation learning for scalability and efficiency.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the scalability bottleneck in IC3/PDR engines caused by repeated graph analysis for inductive generalization during property checking of RTL designs.
AI.method: LeGend pre-trains a self-supervised model to generate global latch embeddings capturing circuit-level properties. These embeddings enable a lightweight model to predict effective lemmas with minimal computational overhead.
AI.result: LeGend accelerates two state-of-the-art IC3/PDR engines across various benchmarks, demonstrating improved efficiency and scalability.
AI.conclusion: The introduction of LeGend illustrates a promising step toward scaling formal verification processes by decoupling computationally expensive learning from inference, improving both performance and scalability.

title: QoSFlow: Ensuring Service Quality of Distributed Workflows Using Interpretable Sensitivity Models
comment: to be published in 40th IEEE International Parallel & Distributed Processing Symposium (IPDPS), 2026
summary: With the increasing importance of distributed scientific workflows, there is a critical need to ensure Quality of Service (QoS) constraints, such as minimizing time or limiting execution to resource subsets. However, the unpredictable nature of workflow behavior, even with similar configurations, makes it difficult to provide QoS guarantees. For effective reasoning about QoS scheduling, we introduce QoSFlow, a performance modeling method that partitions a workflow's execution configuration space into regions with similar behavior. Each region groups configurations with comparable execution times according to a given statistical sensitivity, enabling efficient QoS-driven scheduling through analytical reasoning rather than exhaustive testing. Evaluation on three diverse workflows shows that QoSFlow's execution recommendations outperform the best-performing standard heuristic by 27.38%. Empirical validation confirms that QoSFlow's recommended configurations consistently match measured execution outcomes across different QoS constraints.

AI.tldr: QoSFlow is a performance modeling method for distributed workflows, improving Quality of Service (QoS) constraints by segmenting configurations into similar behavior regions and providing better scheduling recommendations.
AI.motivation: The need to address QoS constraints in distributed scientific workflows, which is challenging due to the unpredictable nature of workflow behavior despite similar configurations.
AI.method: The paper presents QoSFlow, a method that partitions workflows' execution configuration space into behaviorally similar regions based on statistical sensitivity, enabling efficient QoS scheduling without exhaustive testing.
AI.result: QoSFlow outperformed the best standard heuristic by 27.38% in execution time and showed consistent alignment between recommendations and execution results across various QoS constraints.
AI.conclusion: QoSFlow is an effective tool for ensuring QoS in scientific workflows, providing significant improvements in scheduling accuracy and performance.

title: HumanMCP: A Human-Like Query Dataset for Evaluating MCP Tool Retrieval Performance
summary: Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers contain a collection of thousands of open-source standardized tools, linking LLMs to external systems; however, existing datasets and benchmarks lack realistic, human-like user queries, remaining a critical gap in evaluating the tool usage and ecosystems of MCP servers. Existing datasets often do contain tool descriptions but fail to represent how different users portray their requests, leading to poor generalization and inflated reliability of certain benchmarks. This paper introduces the first large-scale MCP dataset featuring diverse, high-quality diverse user queries generated specifically to match 2800 tools across 308 MCP servers, developing on the MCP Zero dataset. Each tool is paired with multiple unique user personas that we have generated, to capture varying levels of user intent ranging from precise task requests, and ambiguous, exploratory commands, reflecting the complexity of real-world interaction patterns.

AI.tldr: MCP servers need better datasets with realistic human-like queries to evaluate tool usage effectively.
AI.motivation: Existing datasets poorly generalize due to lack of realistic user queries tied to tools in MCP servers.
AI.method: Introduced a large-scale MCP dataset with diverse user queries tailored to match tools across MCP servers.
AI.result: The new dataset pairs tools with multiple user personas that reflect real-world interaction patterns.
AI.conclusion: This dataset bridges a gap in evaluating MCP ecosystems by incorporating realistic user-driven queries.

title: Rudder: Steering Prefetching in Distributed GNN Training using LLM Agents
comment: Accepted to the 40th ACM International Conference on Supercomputing (ICS 2026)
summary: Large-scale Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are typically trained by sampling a vertex's neighbors to a fixed distance. Because large input graphs are distributed, training requires frequent irregular communication that stalls forward progress. Moreover, fetched data changes with graph, graph distribution, sample and batch parameters, and caching polices. Consequently, any static prefetching method will miss crucial opportunities to adapt to different dynamic conditions. In this paper, we introduce Rudder, a software module embedded in the state-of-the-art AWS DistDGL framework, to autonomously prefetch remote nodes and minimize communication. Rudder's adaptation contrasts with both standard heuristics and traditional ML classifiers. We observe that the generative AI found in contemporary Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibits emergent properties like In-Context Learning (ICL) for zero-shot tasks, with logical multi-step reasoning. We find this behavior well-suited for adaptive control even with substantial undertraining. Evaluations using standard datasets and unseen configurations on the NERSC Perlmutter supercomputer show up to 91% improvement in end-to-end training performance over baseline DistDGL (no prefetching), and an 82% improvement over static prefetching, reducing communication by over 50%. Our code is available at https://github.com/aishwaryyasarkar/rudder-llm-agent.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Rudder, a software module to autonomously prefetch remote nodes in distributed GNN training, reducing communication overhead and improving training performance.
AI.motivation: Training large-scale GNNs is inefficient due to frequent irregular communication caused by graph distribution and dynamic data access patterns. Static prefetching methods cannot adapt to changing conditions, hindering performance.
AI.method: The paper introduces Rudder, integrated into the AWS DistDGL framework, which employs generative AI (leveraging logical multi-step reasoning and In-Context Learning abilities of LLMs) for dynamic adaptive prefetching to minimize communication.
AI.result: Rudder achieved up to 91% improvement in end-to-end training performance over DistDGL without prefetching, 82% over static prefetching, and reduced communication by over 50% using standard datasets on high-performance systems.
AI.conclusion: Rudder effectively leverages LLM properties for adaptive control in GNN training, significantly enhancing performance and reducing communication overhead. Results demonstrate its ability to dynamically adapt to varying conditions in distributed settings.

title: Speak Now: Safe Actor Programming with Multiparty Session Types
comment: Extended version of paper accepted at OOPSLA'26
summary:
Actor languages such as Erlang and Elixir are widely used for implementing scalable and reliable distributed applications, but the informally-specified nature of actor communication patterns leaves systems vulnerable to costly errors such as communication mismatches and deadlocks. Multiparty session types (MPSTs) rule out communication errors early in the development process, but until now, the many-sender, single-receiver nature of actor communication has made it difficult for actor languages to benefit from session types.
  This paper introduces Maty, the first actor language design supporting both static multiparty session typing and the full power of actors taking part in multiple sessions. Maty therefore combines the error prevention mechanism of session types with the scalability and fault tolerance of actor languages. Our main insight is to enforce session typing through a flow-sensitive effect system, combined with an event-driven programming style and first-class message handlers. Using MPSTs allows us to guarantee communication safety: a process will never send or receive an unexpected message, nor will a session get stuck because an actor is waiting for a message that will never be sent. We extend Maty to support Erlang-style supervision and cascading failure, and show that this preserves Maty's strong metatheory. We implement Maty in Scala using an API generation approach, and demonstrate the expressiveness of our model by implementing a representative sample of the widely-used Savina actor benchmark suite; an industry-supplied factory scenario; and a chat server.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Maty, an actor language design that integrates multiparty session typing with actor communication patterns to prevent communication errors while maintaining the scalability and fault-tolerance of actor languages.
AI.motivation: Existing actor languages such as Erlang and Elixir are highly reliable and scalable, but their informal communication patterns allow costly errors like communication mismatches and deadlocks. Session types could address these errors but have not been adapted effectively for actor systems.
AI.method: The authors propose Maty, a new actor language based on a flow-sensitive effect system, an event-driven programming style, and message handlers to enable multiparty session typing. Maty also integrates Erlang-style supervision and failure handling.
AI.result: Maty guarantees communication safety, ensuring no unexpected messages or session deadlocks occur. The implemented system in Scala demonstrates its expressiveness through benchmarks, an industry use case, and application examples.
AI.conclusion: Maty successfully combines the error prevention of session typing with scalability and fault tolerance of actor languages, advancing rigorous programming practices for distributed systems.

title: On De-Individuated Neurons: Continuous Symmetries Enable Dynamic Topologies
comment: 22 pages, 5 figures, preprint to be submitted for review at Transactions on Machine Learning Research (TMLR)
summary: This paper introduces a novel methodology for dynamic networks by leveraging a new symmetry-principled class of primitives, isotropic activation functions. This approach enables real-time neuronal growth and shrinkage of the architectures in response to task demand. This is made possible by network structural changes that are invariant under symmetry reparameterisations, leaving the computation identical under neurogenesis and well approximated under neurodegeneration. This is undertaken by leveraging the isotropic primitives' property of basis independence, resulting in the loss of the individuated neurons implicit in the elementwise functional form. Isotropy thereby allows a freedom in the basis to which layers are decomposed and interpreted as individual artificial neurons. This enables a layer-wise diagonalisation procedure, in which typical interconnected layers, such as dense layers, convolutional kernels, and others, can be reexpressed so that neurons have one-to-one, ordered connectivity within alternating layers. This indicates which one-to-one neuron-to-neuron communications are strongly impactful on overall functionality and which are not. Inconsequential neurons can thus be removed (neurodegeneration), and new inactive scaffold neurons added (neurogenesis) whilst remaining analytically invariant in function. A new tunable model parameter, intrinsic length, is also introduced to ensure this analytical invariance. This approach mathematically equates connectivity pruning with neurodegeneration. The diagonalisation also offers new possibilities for mechanistic interpretability into isotropic networks, and it is demonstrated that isotropic dense networks can asymptotically reach a sparsity factor of 50% whilst retaining exact network functionality. Finally, the construction is generalised, demonstrating a nested functional class for this form of isotropic primitive architectures.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a novel framework for dynamic neural networks using isotropic activation functions, allowing networks to adapt in size by analytically invariant neurogenesis and neurodegeneration processes.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to create more adaptive neural networks capable of real-time structural adjustments to better meet task requirements while retaining their functionality.
AI.method: The authors introduce isotropic activation functions to neuron layers, enabling flexible reorganization through symmetry principles. They also propose a layer-wise diagonalization procedure to distinguish impactful from inconsequential neurons.
AI.result: The method achieves exact analytical invariance while facilitating neuron addition or removal. Tests show dense isotropic networks attain 50% sparsity without losing functional accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The introduced isotropic primitives and diagonalization offer a foundation for dynamic neural architectures, improving adaptability, pruning efficiency, and mechanistic interpretability.

title: Toward General Semantic Chunking: A Discriminative Framework for Ultra-Long Documents
summary: Long-document topic segmentation plays an important role in information retrieval and document understanding, yet existing methods still show clear shortcomings in ultra-long text settings. Traditional discriminative models are constrained by fixed windows and cannot model document-level semantics; generative large language models can output paragraph boundaries, but inference is expensive and long inputs are difficult to support. To address these issues, we propose a discriminative segmentation model based on Qwen3-0.6B. On top of the backbone network, we add a cross-window context fusion layer and a boundary classification head, and combine them with an overlapping sliding-window strategy. Our model supports single-pass inputs of up to 13k tokens and can be extended to ultra-long documents for paragraph boundary detection. To further enhance downstream retrieval efficiency, we derive a vector fusion method with scalar correction, which compresses the representation of ultra-long segments into a single vector without semantic loss. Experiments on the Wikipedia long-document topic segmentation dataset WIKI-727K show that, compared with three generative models based on Qwen2-0.5B released by Jina, our method achieves a better macro-averaged F1 and delivers two orders of magnitude faster inference, substantially improving the practicality and scalability of long-document processing.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a discriminative model for long-document topic segmentation, incorporating innovations for ultra-long texts, outperforming generative models in efficiency and accuracy.
AI.motivation: Long-document segmentation is crucial but existing methods struggle with efficiency and scalability in ultra-long text settings.
AI.method: A discriminative segmentation model is developed using Qwen3-0.6B with a cross-window context fusion layer, a boundary classification head, and an overlapping sliding-window strategy. A vector fusion method for efficient segment representation is also proposed.
AI.result: The model handles inputs up to 13k tokens in a single pass, achieves better macro-averaged F1 scores, and delivers significantly faster inference compared to state-of-the-art generative models.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method significantly boosts the practicality and scalability of processing long documents for topic segmentation and downstream tasks.

title: SGAgent: Suggestion-Guided LLM-Based Multi-Agent Framework for Repository-Level Software Repair
summary: The rapid advancement of Large Language Models (LLMs) has led to the emergence of intelligent agents capable of autonomously interacting with environments and invoking external tools. Recently, agent-based software repair approaches have received widespread attention, as repair agents can automatically analyze and localize bugs, generate patches, and achieve state-of-the-art performance on repository-level benchmarks. However, existing approaches usually adopt a localize-then-fix paradigm, jumping directly from "where the bug is" to "how to fix it", leaving a fundamental reasoning gap. To this end, we propose SGAgent, a Suggestion-Guided multi-Agent framework for repository-level software repair, which follows a localize-suggest-fix paradigm. SGAgent introduces a suggestion phase to strengthen the transition from localization to repair. The suggester starts from the buggy locations and incrementally retrieves relevant context until it fully understands the bug, and then provides actionable repair suggestions. Moreover, we construct a Knowledge Graph from the target repository and develop a KG-based toolkit to enhance SGAgent's global contextual awareness and repository-level reasoning. Three specialized sub-agents (i.e., localizer, suggester, and fixer) collaborate to achieve automated end-to-end software repair. Experimental results on SWE-Bench show that SGAgent with Claude-3.5 achieves 51.3% repair accuracy, 81.2% file-level and 52.4% function-level localization accuracy with an average cost of $1.48 per instance, outperforming all baselines using the same base model. Furthermore, SGAgent attains 48% accuracy on VUL4J and VJBench for vulnerability repair, demonstrating strong generalization across tasks and programming languages.

AI.tldr: SGAgent introduces a suggestion-guided multi-agent framework to improve software repair workflows, achieving superior bug localization and repair accuracy with impressive cost efficiency across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing software repair methods face a reasoning gap in transitioning from bug localization to repair, as they skip a crucial understanding phase. The SGAgent framework aims to bridge this gap.
AI.method: SGAgent employs a multi-agent framework (localizer, suggester, and fixer) and introduces a suggestion phase. It utilizes Knowledge Graphs for enhanced repository-level reasoning and contextual understanding.
AI.result: SGAgent outperforms baselines, achieving 51.3% repair accuracy, 81.2% file-level localization accuracy, 48% vulnerability repair accuracy, and showing strong generalization across benchmarks and programming languages.
AI.conclusion: SGAgent demonstrates the effectiveness of a suggestion-guided approach and multi-agent collaboration in elevating software repair accuracy and task generalization, reducing costs per instance.

title: Audited calibration under regime shift as a computational test of support-structured broadcast
summary: A central prediction of the accompanying theoretical framework is that metacognitive calibration can vary even when content-level performance is held approximately fixed, depending on whether support structure is preserved in a globally reusable broadcast state. We provide a minimal computational test of this claim using a two-channel probabilistic cue-integration task with regime shifts that induce systematic miscalibration in one channel. We compare content-dominated architectures, in which confidence is calibrated by a single global mapping from evidence strength to probability, to an auditor architecture that learns a regime-conditioned calibration mapping from an audit trail of outcomes. We then couple confidence to control by implementing a policy that either acts immediately or requests one additional sample when confidence falls below a threshold. Across matched evidence streams, the auditor substantially improves calibration, particularly in the degraded regime, and produces qualitatively different control behavior by selectively requesting additional evidence under low-support conditions. These results demonstrate a concrete, testable dissociation between content performance and system-level confidence and policy that arises from globally reusable support summaries.

AI.tldr: The paper studies varying metacognitive calibration under consistent performance levels by testing a theoretical model with a probabilistic cue-integration task. The study highlights differences in confidence and control behavior between different system architectures.
AI.motivation: To understand how metacognitive calibration varies under identical performance conditions and to explore the role of globally reusable support structures in calibration and resulting behavior.
AI.method: The authors use a computational test with a two-channel probabilistic cue-integration task, comparing a content-dominated architecture to an auditor architecture under systematic miscalibration conditions.
AI.result: The auditor architecture significantly improves calibration in degraded conditions and alters control behavior by selectively requesting evidence under low-confidence circumstances.
AI.conclusion: Metacognitive calibration and control behaviors are influenced by the presence of globally reusable support summaries, offering valuable insight into the relationship between system design and calibration.

title: Cybersecurity of Teleoperated Quadruped Robots: A Systematic Survey of Vulnerabilities, Threats, and Open Defense Gaps
summary:
Teleoperated quadruped robots are increasingly deployed in safety-critical missions -- industrial inspection, military reconnaissance, and emergency response -- yet the security of their communication and control infrastructure remains insufficiently characterized. Quadrupeds present distinct security challenges arising from dynamic stability constraints, gait-dependent vulnerability windows, substantial kinetic energy, and elevated operator cognitive load.
  This survey synthesizes peer-reviewed literature and vulnerability disclosures (2019--2025) to provide comprehensive analysis of cybersecurity threats, consequences, and countermeasures for teleoperated quadruped systems. We contribute: (i) a six-layer attack taxonomy spanning perception manipulation, VR/AR operator targeting, communication disruption, control signal attacks, localization spoofing, and network intrusion; (ii) systematic attack-to-consequence mapping with timing characterization; (iii) Technology Readiness Level classification exposing critical maturity gaps between field-deployed communication protections (TRL 7--9) and experimental perception/operator-layer defenses (TRL 3--5); (iv) comparative security analysis of six commercial platforms; (v) pragmatic deployment guidance stratified by implementation timeline; and (vi) eight prioritized research gaps with implementation roadmaps.
  Limitations: Platform assessments rely on publicly available information. Attack success rates derive from cited studies under controlled conditions and require domain-specific validation.

AI.tldr: Teleoperated quadruped robots face unique cybersecurity challenges; this survey provides an attack taxonomy, maturity gap analysis, security comparison, and research priorities.
AI.motivation: Safety-critical missions involving teleoperated quadrupeds demand robust cybersecurity due to their dynamic stability, vulnerability windows, and operator complexities.
AI.method: The survey synthesizes literature, disclosures (2019–2025), and evaluates systems with a taxonomy, security readiness levels, and commercial platform comparisons.
AI.result: The analysis identifies six attack categories, timing consequences, readiness classification gaps, security of deployment on platforms, and eight research priorities.
AI.conclusion: There are significant cybersecurity gaps in teleoperated quadruped systems; further research is needed addressing prioritized vulnerabilities and readiness disparities.

title: DesignSense: A Human Preference Dataset and Reward Modeling Framework for Graphic Layout Generation
summary: Graphic layouts serve as an important and engaging medium for visual communication across different channels. While recent layout generation models have demonstrated impressive capabilities, they frequently fail to align with nuanced human aesthetic judgment. Existing preference datasets and reward models trained on text-to-image generation do not generalize to layout evaluation, where the spatial arrangement of identical elements determines quality. To address this critical gap, we introduce DesignSense-10k, a large-scale dataset of 10,235 human-annotated preference pairs for graphic layout evaluation. We propose a five-stage curation pipeline that generates visually coherent layout transformations across diverse aspect ratios, using semantic grouping, layout prediction, filtering, clustering, and VLM-based refinement to produce high-quality comparison pairs. Human preferences are annotated using a 4-class scheme (left, right, both good, both bad) to capture subjective ambiguity. Leveraging this dataset, we train DesignSense, a vision-language model-based classifier that substantially outperforms existing open-source and proprietary models across comprehensive evaluation metrics (54.6% improvement in Macro F1 over the strongest proprietary baseline). Our analysis shows that frontier VLMs remain unreliable overall and fail catastrophically on the full four-class task, underscoring the need for specialized, preference-aware models. Beyond the dataset, our reward model DesignSense yields tangible downstream gains in layout generation. Using our judge during RL based training improves generator win rate by about 3%, while inference-time scaling, which involves generating multiple candidates and selecting the best one, provides a 3.6% improvement. These results highlight the practical impact of specialized, layout-aware preference modeling on real-world layout generation quality.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DesignSense-10k, a dataset for graphic layout preference modeling, and develops a classifier that significantly outperforms existing models in layout quality evaluation.
AI.motivation: Existing models lack alignment with nuanced human aesthetic judgment in layout generation, and current datasets and reward models are insufficient for layout evaluation.
AI.method: Created DesignSense-10k, a dataset of human-annotated preference pairs using a five-stage curation, and trained DesignSense classifier, leveraging a vision-language model.
AI.result: DesignSense classifier achieved a 54.6% improvement in Macro F1 over proprietary baselines, enhanced generator win rates (+3%) through RL training, and inference-time scaling boosted win rates (+3.6%).
AI.conclusion: Specialized, layout-aware preference models like DesignSense improve layout generation quality and demonstrate practical benefits in aligning aesthetic judgment with human preferences.

title: Detoxifying LLMs via Representation Erasure-Based Preference Optimization
summary: Large language models (LLMs) trained on webscale data can produce toxic outputs, raising concerns for safe deployment. Prior defenses, based on applications of DPO, NPO, and similar algorithms, reduce the likelihood of harmful continuations, but not robustly so: they are vulnerable to adversarial prompting and easily undone by fine-tuning-based relearning attacks. Indeed, research has shown that these edits to the model are superficial: linear probing reveals that harmful "directions" remain present in representations. To address this, we propose Representation Erasure-based Preference Optimization (REPO), reformulating detoxification as a token-level preference problem. Using a novel objective with preference data, we force the representations of toxic continuations to converge toward their benign counterparts. Our mechanistic analysis reveals that this granular approach is critical: unlike baselines, REPO induces deep, localized edits to toxicity-encoding neurons while preserving general model utility. Exhaustive evaluations show that REPO achieves state-of-the-art robustness, stopping sophisticated threats-including relearning attacks and enhanced GCG jailbreaks-where existing representation- and output-based methods fail.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces REPO, a method for robust detoxification of large language models (LLMs) by deeply editing toxicity-encoding neuron representations, achieving superior defense against adversarial and relearning attacks.
AI.motivation: LLMs often produce toxic outputs despite existing defenses, which fail under adversarial prompting or relearning attacks due to superficial edits to the model.
AI.method: REPO reformulates detoxification into a token-level preference optimization problem and forces toxic representations to converge toward benign ones using a novel objective and mechanistic editing of neurons.
AI.result: REPO demonstrates state-of-the-art robustness in detoxifying LLMs, successfully mitigating diverse sophisticated attacks like relearning and Jailbreaking methods.
AI.conclusion: REPO provides a robust, neurologically-focused approach that ensures deep edits to toxic representations while maintaining general model utility, contributing a significant step forward in safely deploying LLMs.

title: Uncovering Physical Drivers of Dark Matter Halo Structures with Auxiliary-Variable-Guided Generative Models
summary: Deep generative models (DGMs) compress high-dimensional data but often entangle distinct physical factors in their latent spaces. We present an auxiliary-variable-guided framework for disentangling representations of thermal Sunyaev-Zel'dovich (tSZ) maps of dark matter halos. We introduce halo mass and concentration as auxiliary variables and apply a lightweight alignment penalty to encourage latent dimensions to reflect these physical quantities. To generate sharp and realistic samples, we extend latent conditional flow matching (LCFM), a state-of-the-art generative model, to enforce disentanglement in the latent space. Our Disentangled Latent-CFM (DL-CFM) model recovers the established mass-concentration scaling relation and identifies latent space outliers that may correspond to unusual halo formation histories. By linking latent coordinates to interpretable astrophysical properties, our method transforms the latent space into a diagnostic tool for cosmological structure. This work demonstrates that auxiliary guidance preserves generative flexibility while yielding physically meaningful, disentangled embeddings, providing a generalizable pathway for uncovering independent factors in complex astronomical datasets.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a novel method for disentangling representations in generative models applied to astronomical datasets, focusing on thermal Sunyaev-Zel'dovich maps.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of latent space entanglement in generative models, particularly in astronomical datasets, and to improve the interpretability of these models by linking latent features to physical properties.
AI.method: An auxiliary-variable-guided framework with halo mass and concentration as auxiliary variables is proposed. It uses an extended latent conditional flow matching (LCFM) approach with an alignment penalty for disentanglement and interpretable latent space.
AI.result: The proposed DL-CFM model successfully recovers known physical relationships such as the mass-concentration scaling and detects latent space outliers related to unusual halo formation, enhancing astrophysical interpretability.
AI.conclusion: The method preserves generative flexibility while achieving physically meaningful disentanglement in latent spaces. It offers a powerful diagnostic tool for studying cosmological structures and independent factors in complex datasets.

title: Shifting in-DRAM
summary: Processing-in-Memory (PIM) architectures enable computation directly within DRAM and help combat the memory wall problem. Bit-shifting is a fundamental operation that enables PIM applications such as shift-and-add multiplication, adders using carry propagation, and Galois field arithmetic used in cryptography algorithms like AES and Reed-Solomon error correction codes. Existing approaches to in-DRAM shifting require adding dedicated shifter circuits beneath the sense amplifiers to enable horizontal data movement across adjacent bitlines or vertical data layouts which store operand bits along a bitline to implement shifts as row-copy operations. In this paper, we propose a novel DRAM subarray design that enables in-DRAM bit-shifting for open-bitline architectures. In this new design, we built upon prior work that introduced a new type of cell used for row migration in asymmetric subarrays, called a "migration cell". We repurpose and extend the functionality by adding a row of migration cells at the top and bottom of each subarray which enables bidirectional bit-shifting within any given row. This new design maintains compatibility with standard DRAM operations. Unlike previous approaches to shifting, our design operates on horizontally-stored data, eliminating the need and overhead of data transposition, and our design leverages the existing cell structures, eliminating the need for additional complex logic and circuitry. We present an evaluation of our design that includes timing and energy analysis using NVMain, circuit-level validation of the in-DRAM shift operation using LTSPICE, and a VLSI layout implementation in Cadence Virtuoso.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a novel DRAM subarray design that enables bidirectional in-DRAM bit-shifting in open-bitline architectures, improving processing-in-memory (PIM) efficiency without requiring complex additional logic.
AI.motivation: Address the need for efficient bit-shifting operations in PIM applications while minimizing the hardware and operational overhead.
AI.method: Introduced a novel DRAM subarray design leveraging migration cells to enable horizontal and bidirectional bit-shifting, eliminating the need for additional transposition or dedicated circuitry.
AI.result: Demonstrated compatibility with standard DRAM operations and validated the design through simulation (NVMain), circuit analysis (LTSPICE), and VLSI implementation (Cadence Virtuoso).
AI.conclusion: The proposed design efficiently enables in-DRAM bit-shifting, overcoming limitations of prior methods, and maintains compatibility with current DRAM standards, paving the way for enhanced PIM architectures.

title: Hestia: Hyperthread-Level Scheduling for Cloud Microservices with Interference-Aware Attention
comment: This paper has been accepted for publication in Design Automation Conference(DAC 2026)
summary: Modern cloud servers routinely co-locate multiple latency-sensitive microservice instances to improve resource efficiency. However, the diversity of microservice behaviors, coupled with mutual performance interference under simultaneous multithreading (SMT), makes large-scale placement increasingly complex. Existing interference aware schedulers and isolation techniques rely on coarse core-level profiling or static resource partitioning, leaving asymmetric hyperthread-level heterogeneity and SMT contention dynamics largely unmodeled. We present Hestia, a hyperthread-level, interference-aware scheduling framework powered by self-attention. Through an extensive analysis of production traces encompassing 32,408 instances across 3,132 servers, we identify two dominant contention patterns -- sharing-core (SC) and sharing-socket (SS) -- and reveal strong asymmetry in their impact. Guided by these insights, Hestia incorporates (1) a self-attention-based CPU usage predictor that models SC/SS contention and hardware heterogeneity, and (2) an interference scoring model that estimates pairwise contention risks to guide scheduling decisions. We evaluate Hestia through large-scale simulation and a real production deployment. Hestia reduces the 95th-percentile service latency by up to 80\%, lowers overall CPU consumption by 2.3\% under the same workload, and surpasses five state-of-the-art schedulers by up to 30.65\% across diverse contention scenarios.

AI.tldr: Hestia is a hyperthread-level interference-aware scheduling framework designed to optimize microservice placement in cloud servers, utilizing self-attention models to handle performance contention.
AI.motivation: Modern cloud servers struggle with resource efficiency due to diverse microservice behaviors and performance interference under simultaneous multithreading (SMT), which is inadequately addressed by existing schedulers and isolation techniques.
AI.method: Hestia incorporates a self-attention-based CPU usage predictor and interference scoring model to analyze and mitigate contention patterns such as sharing-core (SC) and sharing-socket (SS).
AI.result: Hestia achieves an 80% reduction in 95th-percentile service latency, lowers CPU consumption by 2.3%, and performs up to 30.65% better than state-of-the-art schedulers in contention management.
AI.conclusion: By addressing hyperthread-level heterogeneity and contention through advanced prediction and scheduling models, Hestia significantly enhances microservice placement efficiency and reduces latency in cloud environments.

title: An Agentic LLM Framework for Adverse Media Screening in AML Compliance
summary: Adverse media screening is a critical component of anti-money laundering (AML) and know-your-customer (KYC) compliance processes in financial institutions. Traditional approaches rely on keyword-based searches that generate high false-positive rates or require extensive manual review. We present an agentic system that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to automate adverse media screening. Our system implements a multi-step approach where an LLM agent searches the web, retrieves and processes relevant documents, and computes an Adverse Media Index (AMI) score for each subject. We evaluate our approach using multiple LLM backends on a dataset comprising Politically Exposed Persons (PEPs), persons from regulatory watchlists, and sanctioned persons from OpenSanctions and clean names from academic sources, demonstrating the system's ability to distinguish between high-risk and low-risk individuals.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a system leveraging Large Language Models (LLMs) and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) for automated adverse media screening in anti-money laundering and KYC compliance.
AI.motivation: To address inefficiencies and high false-positive rates in traditional keyword-based adverse media screening approaches in AML and KYC processes.
AI.method: The authors developed a multi-step agentic system utilizing LLMs with RAG, involving web searches, document processing, and an Adverse Media Index (AMI) scoring system.
AI.result: The system was tested on a dataset including Politically Exposed Persons, watchlist individuals, and sanctioned persons, proving its effectiveness in distinguishing high-risk and low-risk entities.
AI.conclusion: The proposed system demonstrates the potential of using advanced AI techniques to improve adverse media screening, automating the process and reducing manual intervention while increasing accuracy.

title: CIll: CTI-Guided Invariant Generation via LLMs for Model Checking
summary: Inductive invariants are crucial in model checking, yet generating effective inductive invariants automatically and efficiently remains challenging. A common approach is to iteratively analyze counterexamples to induction (CTIs) and derive invariants that rule them out, as in IC3. However, IC3's clause-based learning is limited to a CNF representation. For some designs, the resulting invariants may require a large number of clauses, which hurts scalability. We present CIll, a CTI-guided framework that leverages LLMs to synthesize invariants for model checking. CIll alternates between (bounded) correctness checking and inductiveness checking for the generated invariants. In correctness checking, CIll uses BMC to validate whether the generated invariants hold on reachable states within a given bound. In inductiveness checking, CIll checks whether the generated invariants, together with the target property, become inductive under the accumulated strengthening. When inductiveness fails, CIll extracts CTIs and provides them to the LLM. The LLM inspects the design and the CTI to propose new invariants that invalidate the CTIs. The proposed invariants are then re-validated through correctness and inductiveness checks, and the loop continues until the original property strengthened by the generated invariants becomes inductive. CIll also employs IC3 to work with the LLM for automatically discovering invariants, and uses K-Induction as a complementary engine. To improve performance, CIll applies local proof and reuses invariants learned by IC3, reducing redundant search and accelerating convergence. In our evaluation, CIll proved full compliance within RISCV-Formal framework and full accuracy of all non-M instructions in NERV and PicoRV32, whereas M extensions are proved against the RVFI ALTOPS substitute semantics provided by RISCV-Formal.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces CIll, a novel framework utilizing LLMs for the efficient generation of inductive invariants in model checking, overcoming the limitations of IC3's CNF representation.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of IC3's clause-based invariant generation, particularly its inefficiency with designs requiring numerous clauses, and to improve scalability in model checking processes.
AI.method: CIll alternates between bounded correctness checking and inductiveness checking. It incorporates CTI-guided invariant generation, utilizes LLMs to propose new invariants, collaborates with IC3 for automatic discovery, and employs K-Induction as a complementary method while optimizing performance via local proof and invariant reuse.
AI.result: The CIll framework demonstrated success in proving full compliance within the RISCV-Formal framework and verified the full accuracy of NERV and PicoRV32 instructions, with partial extensions tested against substitute semantics.
AI.conclusion: LLM-assisted invariant synthesis, as implemented in CIll, shows significant potential to overcome the computational limitations of traditional methods like IC3, offering improved efficiency and scalability in model checking applications.

title: All Mutation Rates $c/n$ for the $(1+1)$ Evolutionary Algorithm
summary: For every real number $c \geq 1$ and for all $\varepsilon > 0$, there is a fitness function $f : \{0,1\}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ for which the optimal mutation rate for the $(1+1)$ evolutionary algorithm on $f$, denoted $p_n$, satisfies $p_n \approx c/n$ in that $|np_n - c| < \varepsilon$. In other words, the set of all $c \geq 1$ for which the mutation rate $c/n$ is optimal for the $(1+1)$ EA is dense in the interval $[1, \infty)$. To show this, a fitness function is introduced which is called HillPathJump.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a fitness function, HillPathJump, to demonstrate that for any real $c \geq 1$, there exists a corresponding mutation rate $c/n$ that is optimal for the $(1+1)$ evolutionary algorithm on this fitness function.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to explore the optimal mutation rates for evolutionary algorithms, particularly showing the flexibility and range of rates that can achieve optimal performance in this context.
AI.method: The authors define a custom fitness function named HillPathJump to analyze and establish the relationship between mutation rates and algorithmic performance, proving that mutation rates of the form $c/n$ are dense in the interval $[1, \infty)$.
AI.result: The results indicate that for any $c \geq 1$ and small $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists a fitness function where the optimal mutation rate satisfies $p_n \approx c/n$ with high precision.
AI.conclusion: The study reinforces the adaptability of mutation rates in the $(1+1)$ evolutionary algorithm, highlighting that optimal mutation rates span over a dense range, depending on the fitness function.

title: Task-Lens: Cross-Task Utility Based Speech Dataset Profiling for Low-Resource Indian Languages
summary: The rising demand for inclusive speech technologies amplifies the need for multilingual datasets for Natural Language Processing (NLP) research. However, limited awareness of existing task-specific resources in low-resource languages hinders research. This challenge is especially acute in linguistically diverse countries, such as India. Cross-task profiling of existing Indian speech datasets can alleviate the data scarcity challenge. This involves investigating the utility of datasets across multiple downstream tasks rather than focusing on a single task. Prior surveys typically catalogue datasets for a single task, leaving comprehensive cross-task profiling as an open opportunity. Therefore, we propose Task-Lens, a cross-task survey that assesses the readiness of 50 Indian speech datasets spanning 26 languages for nine downstream speech tasks. First, we analyze which datasets contain metadata and properties suitable for specific tasks. Next, we propose task-aligned enhancements to unlock datasets to their full downstream potential. Finally, we identify tasks and Indian languages that are critically underserved by current resources. Our findings reveal that many Indian speech datasets contain untapped metadata that can support multiple downstream tasks. By uncovering cross-task linkages and gaps, Task-Lens enables researchers to explore the broader applicability of existing datasets and to prioritize dataset creation for underserved tasks and languages.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the shortage of multilingual NLP datasets for low-resource languages by assessing the suitability of 50 Indian speech datasets for various tasks and offering recommendations for improvement.
AI.motivation: To overcome the lack of awareness and access to task-specific, multilingual NLP datasets, especially for low-resource languages in diverse countries like India.
AI.method: The authors evaluated 50 Indian speech datasets across 26 languages for nine downstream tasks, analyzed metadata, proposed enhancements, and identified underserved tasks and languages.
AI.result: The evaluation revealed untapped metadata in current datasets that can be used for multiple tasks, gaps in language and task coverage, and provided task-aligned recommendations for improvement.
AI.conclusion: Task-Lens aids researchers in better utilizing existing datasets' potential and encourages prioritizing the development of resources for underserved languages and tasks.

title: Peeling Off the Cocoon: Unveiling Suppressed Golden Seeds for Mutational Greybox Fuzzing
summary: PoCo is a technique that aims to enhance modern coverage-based seed selection (CSS) techniques (such as afl-cmin) by gradually removing obstacle conditional statements and conducting deeper seed selection.

AI.tldr: PoCo enhances seed selection techniques like afl-cmin by removing obstacle conditional statements and refining selection.
AI.motivation: Modern CSS techniques require improved methodologies to overcome obstacles and achieve deeper seed evaluation.
AI.method: It removes obstacle conditional statements incrementally, refining seed selection effectively.
AI.result: Improved coverage-based seed selection techniques for deeper examination.
AI.conclusion: PoCo optimizes CSS techniques for better performance in seed selection and analysis.

title: Brain-OF: An Omnifunctional Foundation Model for fMRI, EEG and MEG
summary: Brain foundation models have achieved remarkable advances across a wide range of neuroscience tasks. However, most existing models are limited to a single functional modality, restricting their ability to exploit complementary spatiotemporal dynamics and the collective data scale across imaging techniques. To address this limitation, we propose Brain-OF, the first omnifunctional brain foundation model jointly pretrained on fMRI, EEG and MEG, capable of handling both unimodal and multimodal inputs within a unified framework. To reconcile heterogeneous spatiotemporal resolutions, we introduce the Any-Resolution Neural Signal Sampler, which projects diverse brain signals into a shared semantic space.To further manage semantic shifts, the Brain-OF backbone integrates DINT attention with a Sparse Mixture of Experts, where shared experts capture modality-invariant representations and routed experts specialize in modality-specific semantics. Furthermore, we propose Masked Temporal-Frequency Modeling, a dual-domain pretraining objective that jointly reconstructs brain signals in both the time and frequency domains. Brain-OF is pretrained on a large-scale corpus comprising around 40 datasets and demonstrates superior performance across diverse downstream tasks, highlighting the benefits of joint multimodal integration and dual-domain pretraining.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Brain-OF, an omnifunctional brain foundation model that jointly pretrains on multiple neuroimaging modalities (fMRI, EEG, and MEG) to handle both unimodal and multimodal inputs, achieving superior results in diverse neuroscience tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing brain foundation models are limited to single modalities, restricting their ability to leverage the complementary spatiotemporal dynamics and large-scale data across different imaging techniques.
AI.method: Brain-OF employs a unified framework with innovations like the Any-Resolution Neural Signal Sampler, a backbone combining DINT attention and Sparse Mixture of Experts, and Masked Temporal-Frequency Modeling for dual-domain pretraining on time and frequency signals.
AI.result: Brain-OF was pretrained on a dataset of about 40 corpora and exhibited superior performance on various neuroscience downstream tasks, demonstrating the benefits of multimodal integration and dual-domain pretraining.
AI.conclusion: The model effectively overcomes limitations of single-modality approaches, proving the potential of joint multimodal and dual-domain strategies for advancing neuroscience applications.

title: Demystifying Action Space Design for Robotic Manipulation Policies
summary: The specification of the action space plays a pivotal role in imitation-based robotic manipulation policy learning, fundamentally shaping the optimization landscape of policy learning. While recent advances have focused heavily on scaling training data and model capacity, the choice of action space remains guided by ad-hoc heuristics or legacy designs, leading to an ambiguous understanding of robotic policy design philosophies. To address this ambiguity, we conducted a large-scale and systematic empirical study, confirming that the action space does have significant and complex impacts on robotic policy learning. We dissect the action design space along temporal and spatial axes, facilitating a structured analysis of how these choices govern both policy learnability and control stability. Based on 13,000+ real-world rollouts on a bimanual robot and evaluation on 500+ trained models over four scenarios, we examine the trade-offs between absolute vs. delta representations, and joint-space vs. task-space parameterizations. Our large-scale results suggest that properly designing the policy to predict delta actions consistently improves performance, while joint-space and task-space representations offer complementary strengths, favoring control stability and generalization, respectively.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically studies the impact of action space design choices on imitation-based robotic manipulation policy learning, revealing significant and complex effects.
AI.motivation: The study aims to address the lack of understanding and ad-hoc practices in the design of action spaces for robotic policy learning, which significantly influences policy learnability and control stability.
AI.method: A large-scale empirical study was conducted involving 13,000+ real-world rollouts on a bimanual robot and evaluation of 500+ trained models across four scenarios, analyzing temporal and spatial action design trade-offs.
AI.result: The study found that delta actions significantly improve performance while joint-space and task-space representations offer complementary benefits, focusing on control stability and generalization, respectively.
AI.conclusion: Action space design critically shapes robotic policy learning outcomes. Properly designed policies that predict delta actions improve performance, and combining joint-space and task-space representations may optimize results depending on task goals.

title: Modelling and Simulation of Neuromorphic Datasets for Anomaly Detection in Computer Vision
summary: Limitations on the availability of Dynamic Vision Sensors (DVS) present a fundamental challenge to researchers of neuromorphic computer vision applications. In response, datasets have been created by the research community, but often contain a limited number of samples or scenarios. To address the lack of a comprehensive simulator of neuromorphic vision datasets, we introduce the Anomalous Neuromorphic Tool for Shapes (ANTShapes), a novel dataset simulation framework. Built in the Unity engine, ANTShapes simulates abstract, configurable 3D scenes populated by objects displaying randomly-generated behaviours describing attributes such as motion and rotation. The sampling of object behaviours, and the labelling of anomalously-acting objects, is a statistical process following central limit theorem principles. Datasets containing an arbitrary number of samples can be created and exported from ANTShapes, along with accompanying label and frame data, through the adjustment of a limited number of parameters within the software. ANTShapes addresses the limitations of data availability to researchers of event-based computer vision by allowing for the simulation of bespoke datasets to suit purposes including object recognition and localisation alongside anomaly detection.

AI.tldr: This paper presents ANTShapes, a simulator for neuromorphic vision datasets built in Unity, which allows for rapid and customizable generation of event-based datasets, addressing the lack of dataset availability for neuromorphic computer vision research.
AI.motivation: The research community lacks a comprehensive simulator for neuromorphic vision datasets, as existing datasets are limited in samples or scenarios, hindering advancements in event-based computer vision research.
AI.method: The authors developed the ANTShapes simulation framework using the Unity engine, creating configurable 3D scenes with randomly-generated object behaviors (e.g., motion, rotation). The behaviors follow statistical processes, enabling users to customize and produce datasets with labeled anomalies and frame data.
AI.result: ANTShapes facilitates the creation of large, bespoke datasets with labeled anomalous behaviors, addressing issues related to data scarcity for neuromorphic vision research.
AI.conclusion: ANTShapes provides a valuable tool for generating event-based vision datasets, advancing research in applications such as anomaly detection, object recognition, and localization.

title: U-CAN: Utility-Aware Contrastive Attenuation for Efficient Unlearning in Generative Recommendation
summary: Generative Recommendation (GenRec) typically leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) to redefine personalization as an instruction-driven sequence generation task. However, fine-tuning on user logs inadvertently encodes sensitive attributes into model parameters, raising critical privacy concerns. Existing Machine Unlearning (MU) techniques struggle to navigate this tension due to the Polysemy Dilemma, where neurons superimpose sensitive data with general reasoning patterns, leading to catastrophic utility loss under traditional gradient or pruning methods. To address this, we propose Utility-aware Contrastive AttenuatioN (U-CAN), a precision unlearning framework that operates on low-rank adapters. U-CAN quantifies risk by contrasting activations and focuses on neurons with asymmetric responses that are highly sensitive to the forgetting set but suppressed on the retention set. To safeguard performance, we introduce a utility-aware calibration mechanism that combines weight magnitudes with retention-set activation norms, assigning higher utility scores to dimensions that contribute strongly to retention performance. Unlike binary pruning, which often fragments network structure, U-CAN develop adaptive soft attenuation with a differentiable decay function to selectively down-scale high-risk parameters on LoRA adapters, suppressing sensitive retrieval pathways and preserving the topological connectivity of reasoning circuits. Experiments on two public datasets across seven metrics demonstrate that U-CAN achieves strong privacy forgetting, utility retention, and computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces U-CAN, a novel framework for precise unlearning in Generative Recommendation systems, addressing privacy concerns while retaining utility and computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: Motivated by privacy concerns in Generative Recommendation systems, where fine-tuning LLMs unintentionally stores sensitive information in model parameters, leading to challenges for existing Machine Unlearning methods.
AI.method: The U-CAN framework employs low-rank adapters with contrastive attenuation to identify and suppress sensitive neurons based on asymmetric activations. It uses a utility-aware calibration mechanism to retain performance and differentiable decay functions for adaptive attenuation without network fragmentation.
AI.result: The experiments on two datasets demonstrate strong privacy forgetting, high utility retention, and computational efficiency across multiple evaluation metrics.
AI.conclusion: U-CAN effectively balances privacy preservation and utility maintenance in LLMs, offering a computationally efficient solution for safe personalization tasks in GenRec systems.

title: Partition Function Estimation under Bounded f-Divergence
summary: We study the statistical complexity of estimating partition functions given sample access to a proposal distribution and an unnormalized density ratio for a target distribution. While partition function estimation is a classical problem, existing guarantees typically rely on structural assumptions about the domain or model geometry. We instead provide a general, information-theoretic characterization that depends only on the relationship between the proposal and target distributions. Our analysis introduces the integrated coverage profile, a functional that quantifies how much target mass lies in regions where the density ratio is large. We show that integrated coverage tightly characterizes the sample complexity of multiplicative partition function estimation and provide matching lower bounds. We further express these bounds in terms of $f$-divergences, yielding sharp phase transitions depending on the growth rate of f and recovering classical results as a special case while extending to heavy-tailed regimes. Matching lower bounds establish tightness in all regimes. As applications, we derive improved finite-sample guarantees for importance sampling and self-normalized importance sampling, and we show a strict separation between the complexity of approximate sampling and counting under the same divergence constraints. Our results unify and generalize prior analyses of importance sampling, rejection sampling, and heavy-tailed mean estimation, providing a minimal-assumption theory of partition function estimation. Along the way we introduce new technical tools including new connections between coverage and $f$-divergences as well as a generalization of the classical Paley-Zygmund inequality.

AI.tldr: The paper studies the statistical complexity of estimating partition functions with minimal assumptions using a new functional, integrated coverage profile, and connects it with $f$-divergences.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for partition function estimation often depend on structural assumptions. This paper aims to provide a general, assumption-light, information-theoretic framework for the problem.
AI.method: The authors introduce the integrated coverage profile to measure the relationship between the proposal and target distributions and use it to characterize the sample complexity and develop matching lower bounds. Additionally, they connect these results to $f$-divergences and extend the analysis to different regimes.
AI.result: The paper derives improved guarantees for importance sampling methods and demonstrates sharp phase transitions in sample complexity under various growth rates of $f$. It also shows a distinct separation between sampling and counting complexities under divergence constraints.
AI.conclusion: The results unify and generalize prior sampling and estimation analyses, providing a minimal-assumption framework for partition function estimation and introducing valuable tools like generalized $f$-divergence connections and inequalities.

title: PDF: PUF-based DNN Fingerprinting for Knowledge Distillation Traceability
summary: Knowledge distillation transfers large teacher models to compact student models, enabling deployment on resource-limited platforms while suffering minimal performance degradation. However, this paradigm could lead to various security risks, especially model theft. Existing defenses against model theft, such as watermarking and secure enclaves, focus primarily on identity authentication and incur significant resource costs. Aiming to provide post-theft accountability and traceability, we propose a novel fingerprinting framework that superimposes device-specific Physical Unclonable Function (PUF) signatures onto teacher logits during distillation. Compared with watermarking or secure enclaves, our approach is lightweight, requires no architectural changes, and enables traceability of any leaked or cloned model. Since the signatures are based on PUFs, this framework is robust against reverse engineering and tampering attacks. In this framework, the signature recovery process consists of two stages: first a neural network-based decoder and then a Hamming distance decoder. Furthermore, we also propose a bit compression scheme to support a large number of devices. Experiment results demonstrate that our framework achieves high key recovery rate and negligible accuracy loss while allowing a tunable trade-off between these two key metrics. These results show that the proposed framework is a practical and robust solution for protecting distilled models.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a fingerprinting framework using Physical Unclonable Function (PUF) signatures to protect knowledge-distilled models against theft, enabling accountability and traceability of cloned models with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to mitigate security risks, like model theft, associated with knowledge distillation frameworks by providing post-theft accountability and traceability measures.
AI.method: The method involves integrating device-specific PUF signatures into teacher logits during distillation and using a two-stage signature recovery process (neural network-based decoder and Hamming distance decoder), along with a bit compression scheme for scalability.
AI.result: Experiment results demonstrate high key recovery rate, negligible accuracy loss, and a tunable trade-off for protection efficiency, showing the framework's practicality and robustness.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework offers a lightweight and effective solution for protecting distilled models against model theft, providing traceability while maintaining performance.

title: Mixed Choice in Asynchronous Multiparty Session Types
summary: We present a multiparty session type (MST) framework with asynchronous mixed choice (MC). We propose a core construct for MC that allows transient inconsistencies in protocol state between distributed participants, but ensures all participants can always eventually reach a mutually consistent state. We prove the correctness of our system by establishing a progress property and an operational correspondence between global types and distributed local type projections. Based on our theory, we implement a practical toolchain for specifying and validating asynchronous MST protocols featuring MC, and programming compliant gen_statem processes in Erlang/OTP. We test our framework by using our toolchain to specify and reimplement part of the amqp_client of the RabbitMQ broker for Erlang.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework for multiparty session types with asynchronous mixed choice, ensuring consistency in protocol states and demonstrating its application in Erlang.
AI.motivation: The researchers aim to address the challenge of transient inconsistencies in protocol states among distributed participants while ensuring eventual mutual consistency.
AI.method: They propose a core construct for asynchronous mixed choice, prove system correctness with a progress property and operational correspondence, and implement a practical toolchain for protocol validation and coding in Erlang/OTP.
AI.result: The framework is tested by specifying and reimplementing part of RabbitMQ's amqp_client, demonstrating its practical applicability.
AI.conclusion: The theoretical and practical contributions show the framework's robustness in handling asynchronous multiparty protocols with mixed choice and its usefulness in real-world scenarios.

title: Causal Identification from Counterfactual Data: Completeness and Bounding Results
summary: Previous work establishing completeness results for $\textit{counterfactual identification}$ has been circumscribed to the setting where the input data belongs to observational or interventional distributions (Layers 1 and 2 of Pearl's Causal Hierarchy), since it was generally presumed impossible to obtain data from counterfactual distributions, which belong to Layer 3. However, recent work (Raghavan & Bareinboim, 2025) has formally characterized a family of counterfactual distributions which can be directly estimated via experimental methods - a notion they call $\textit{counterfactual realizabilty}$. This leaves open the question of what $\textit{additional}$ counterfactual quantities now become identifiable, given this new access to (some) Layer 3 data. To answer this question, we develop the CTFIDU+ algorithm for identifying counterfactual queries from an arbitrary set of Layer 3 distributions, and prove that it is complete for this task. Building on this, we establish the theoretical limit of which counterfactuals can be identified from physically realizable distributions, thus implying the $\textit{fundamental limit to exact causal inference in the non-parametric setting}$. Finally, given the impossibility of identifying certain critical types of counterfactuals, we derive novel analytic bounds for such quantities using realizable counterfactual data, and corroborate using simulations that counterfactual data helps tighten the bounds for non-identifiable quantities in practice.

AI.tldr: This paper develops the CTFIDU+ algorithm for identifying counterfactual queries using Layer 3 distributions and establishes theoretical limits of counterfactual identification, with bounds provided for non-identifiable quantities.
AI.motivation: Recent advancements show that some counterfactual distributions (Layer 3 data) are physically realizable via experimental methods, raising the question of expanded identification capabilities.
AI.method: The authors introduce the CTFIDU+ algorithm for identifying counterfactual queries from Layer 3 data and demonstrate its completeness for the task. This involves proving theoretical limits and deriving analytic bounds.
AI.result: CTFIDU+ enables identification of more counterfactuals from realizable Layer 3 distributions. Analytic bounds for critical, non-identifiable counterfactual quantities are derived and validated through simulations.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes fundamental limits of causal inference in the non-parametric setting when using realizable counterfactual distributions, contributing to tighter bounds for non-identifiable queries.

title: Green or Fast? Learning to Balance Cold Starts and Idle Carbon in Serverless Computing
summary: Serverless computing simplifies cloud deployment but introduces new challenges in managing service latency and carbon emissions. Reducing cold-start latency requires retaining warm function instances, while minimizing carbon emissions favors reclaiming idle resources. This balance is further complicated by time-varying grid carbon intensity and varying workload patterns, under which static keep-alive policies are inefficient. We present LACE-RL, a latency-aware and carbon-efficient management framework that formulates serverless pod retention as a sequential decision problem. LACE-RL uses deep reinforcement learning to dynamically tune keep-alive durations, jointly modeling cold-start probability, function-specific latency costs, and real-time carbon intensity. Using the Huawei Public Cloud Trace, we show that LACE-RL reduces cold starts by 51.69% and idle keep-alive carbon emissions by 77.08% compared to Huawei's static policy, while achieving better latency-carbon trade-offs than state-of-the-art heuristic and single-objective baselines, approaching Oracle performance.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces LACE-RL, a framework using deep reinforcement learning to optimize serverless pod management by balancing latency and carbon efficiency.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the conflicting challenges of reducing service latency and minimizing carbon emissions in serverless computing, aggravated by dynamic factors like time-varying grid carbon intensity and workload patterns.
AI.method: It proposes LACE-RL, a framework that uses deep reinforcement learning to dynamically adjust keep-alive durations of serverless pods based on cold-start probability, latency costs, and real-time carbon intensity.
AI.result: LACE-RL achieved significant improvements, reducing cold starts by 51.69% and idle carbon emissions by 77.08%, outperforming Huawei's static policy and other heuristic/single-objective baselines.
AI.conclusion: LACE-RL provides an effective solution to the latency-carbon trade-off problem in serverless computing, achieving near-optimal performance under real-world workload scenarios.

title: Zero-Incoherence Capacity of Interactive Encoding Systems: Achievability, Converse, and Side Information Bounds
comment: Lean 4 artifact: 3378 lines, 176 theorems/lemmas across 24 files (0 sorry placeholders). Full formalization available at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18141365
summary:
We introduce the zero-incoherence capacity for interactive multi-location encoding systems: the maximum encoding rate that guarantees exactly zero probability of disagreement among replicated encodings. Our main information-theoretic results are compact and self-contained: an exact capacity theorem ($C_0=1$), a tight side-information lower bound for resolution ($\geq\log_2 k$ bits for $k$-way incoherence), and a rate--complexity separation (modification cost $O(1)$ at capacity vs $Ω(n)$ above).
  The paper frames encoding locations as terminals in a multi-terminal source-coding model.
